title: Ray Diagrams for Thin Converging Lenses — Hard

questions.question: A slide projector forms a magnified image on a screen $2.0\text{ m}$ away. If the slide is $5.0\text{ cm}$ from the lens, what must be true about the focal length of the lens?
options: The focal length is slightly less than $5.0\text{ cm}$.
The focal length is exactly $5.0\text{ cm}$.
The focal length is greater than $5.0\text{ cm}$.
The focal length is exactly $2.0\text{ m}$.
questions.explanation:
A projector forms a real magnified image when the object is between $f$ and $2f$. Thus $f < 5.0\text{ cm} < 2f$, meaning $f$ is less than $5.0\text{ cm}$. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking $f$ must be greater than the object distance, which would produce a virtual image.

questions.question: In a ray diagram, three specific rays are often drawn to locate an image. Which of the following rays is NOT correctly described?
options: A ray through the focal point on the object side refracts to be parallel to the axis.
A ray parallel to the principal axis refracts through the principal focus.
A ray through the optical centre passes straight through.
A ray through the focal point on the object side refracts through the focal point on the image side.
questions.explanation:
A ray passing through the focal point ($F$) on the object side refracts to become parallel to the principal axis, not through the other focal point. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking all rays eventually pass through a focal point.

questions.question: A student views a virtual image through a converging lens. When she moves the object further from the lens (but still within the focal length), how does the image change?
options: The image remains virtual but becomes more magnified.
The image becomes real and inverted.
The image remains virtual but becomes diminished.
The image disappears completely.
questions.explanation:
As $u$ increases toward $f$ while remaining less than $f$, the virtual image produced by a magnifying glass becomes larger and moves further away. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the image becomes real as soon as the object moves.

questions.question: An object of height $2\text{ cm}$ is placed $30\text{ cm}$ from a lens with $f = 10\text{ cm}$. Calculate the height of the real image formed if it is $15\text{ cm}$ from the lens.
options: $1\text{ cm}$
$4\text{ cm}$
$2\text{ cm}$
$0.5\text{ cm}$
questions.explanation:
Magnification $M = v / u = 15 / 30 = 0.5$. Image height $= 2\text{ cm} \times 0.5 = 1\text{ cm}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying by 2 ($v/u$ inverted), leading to $4\text{ cm}$.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of conditions will produce an image that can be captured on a piece of paper?
options: $u = 1.5f$ and $u = 3f$
$u = 0.8f$ only
$u = 2f$ and $u = 0.5f$
questions.explanation:
Real images can be captured on a screen; these occur when the object distance $u > f$. Both $1.5f$ and $3f$ satisfy this. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking virtual images ($u < f$) can be projected onto paper.
